questions.question: Why do API safety defaults belong in system design rather than only in documentation?
options: Because client misuse and abusive patterns are predictable system workloads that shape reliability and cost.
Because documentation automatically enforces runtime limits.
Because only public APIs need any validation rules.
Because safe defaults eliminate the need for authentication.
questions.explanation: Unsafe contract defaults become operational problems under real traffic.

questions.question: Which default is usually safest for a list endpoint?
options: An uncapped page size so clients can fetch as much as they want.
A modest default limit with an explicit maximum cap.
A mandatory full export for every query.
No filtering restrictions of any kind.
questions.explanation: Bounded defaults keep backend work predictable while still serving common usage.

questions.question: What is the main risk of ambiguous retry guidance on write APIs?
options: Clients may retry unsafe operations and amplify duplicate side effects during incidents.
The API will no longer support authentication.
Pagination will become impossible to implement.
Versioning telemetry will stop working.
questions.explanation: If clients cannot tell what is retry-safe, failures often get amplified instead of contained.

questions.question: Which metric most directly shows whether a safety policy is actively protecting the system?
options: Rejected requests by policy type and per-tenant rate-limit hits.
The number of README edits this month.
How many architecture diagrams include arrows.
The total amount of whitespace in API payload samples.
questions.explanation: Those signals show which protections are actually firing and under what pressure.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with strict default parameter caps?
options: They keep capacity predictable, but some legitimate clients may need exceptions or async alternatives.
They eliminate all need for rate limiting.
They guarantee that every query becomes cheap.
They make observability unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Strict caps are protective, but they must be paired with a path for legitimate high-volume use.

questions.question: What is the strongest default for authorization scopes on a shared API?
options: Least-privilege scopes mapped to specific actions and resource boundaries.
One broad super-token for simplicity across every client.
No scopes as long as requests are rate limited.
Scopes chosen only after the API is widely adopted.
questions.explanation: Scope design directly affects blast radius and misuse potential.

questions.question: If large exports are expensive but some customers need them, what is the best contract design?
options: Force all list endpoints to return unlimited results.
Provide a bounded interactive API and a separate asynchronous export workflow.
Disable filtering entirely.
Let the client repeat paginated requests until the backend times out.
questions.explanation: Separate interactive and bulk paths so normal usage stays safe.

questions.question: What failure pattern suggests your safety defaults may be too strict rather than too loose?
options: Healthy clients are repeatedly rejected for common legitimate workflows, and the rejection metrics stay high.
Backend latency spikes because queries are unbounded.
Duplicate writes rise during network incidents.
Tenants bypass rate limits with broad tokens.
questions.explanation: Rejections against normal expected use are a sign the policy needs tuning.
